text.title: Procurement Process and Contract Types
text.summary: Six-step procurement process: make-or-buy, SOW definition, contract type selection, vendor sourcing, negotiation, and ongoing management.

section.title: Step 1: Make-or-Buy Decision

rule.statement: Always include the "build internally" or "do nothing" option as an explicit row in the evaluation matrix; otherwise procurement becomes a forced choice.
rule.rationale: Without a baseline comparison, the decision optimizes vendor selection rather than whether to engage a vendor at all.

section.p: Factors favouring Make: full control needed, internal expertise sufficient, long-term strategic asset. Factors favouring Buy: faster time-to-capability, specialized expertise needed, risk transfer preferred.

rule.statement: Select contract type based on scope clarity and risk allocation: Fixed Price for well-defined scope (vendor bears cost risk); T&M for unclear scope (buyer bears cost risk); Cost Plus for research or highly uncertain work (buyer bears all cost risk).
rule.rationale: Mismatched contract type is the primary cause of cost overruns and disputes; variable scope on a fixed-price contract guarantees conflict.

rule.statement: For T&M contracts, enforce a hard cap and a weekly burn-rate report; trigger a change request at 70% of cap.
rule.rationale: T&M without a cap produces silent budget overruns; the 70% trigger provides time to re-scope or stop before the cap is hit.

example.note: Mobile app development with clear deliverables (design mockups, working app, source code, App Store approval, 95% crash-free): Fixed Price. Cloud infrastructure setup with evolving requirements: T&M with $15K/mo cap and senior/junior rate card.

section.title: Step 3: Sourcing Methods
section.p: RFI: gather market info before committing to a procurement. RFP: formal proposals with solutions (use for complex work). RFQ: price quotes for defined work (use when scope is clear). Sole source: direct engagement — requires documented justification.

rule.statement: Publish the scoring rubric with weights before proposals arrive. Retroactive weight changes after seeing proposals are bias laundering.
rule.rationale: Pre-published weights force the evaluating team to commit to priorities before they are influenced by vendor positioning.

rule.statement: Every contract must include all of the following before signing: IP assignment, sub-processor disclosure, DPA (if PII involved), termination-for-convenience clause with reasonable notice, audit right, SLA with credits, indemnity including AI-output IP, warranty period. Reject any contract missing a clause; do not accept "TBD".
rule.rationale: Missing clauses surface as disputes; an undiscovered IP gap or absent termination clause locks the buyer into a bad vendor indefinitely.

rule.statement: Tie payments to acceptance of deliverables, not calendar dates. Default structure: 25% at start, 50% at milestone, 25% at completion for Fixed Price.
rule.rationale: Calendar-based payments reward delivery timing, not delivery quality; milestone-gated payments preserve leverage for final acceptance.
